skill.name: caveman
skill.description: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage approximately 75 percent by using terse, direct prose while maintaining full technical accuracy. Supports multiple intensity levels and auto-resumes normal language for security warnings and critical confirmations.
skill.summary: AI communication skill that reduces output tokens by 65 to 75 percent using compressed, direct language while preserving technical precision.
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: All 11 static analyzer findings confirmed as false positives. The scanner incorrectly flagged markdown backtick code formatting as Ruby or shell backtick execution at nine locations. Two weak cryptography detections were also false positives, matching unrelated text patterns in YAML frontmatter and markdown headings. The skill is a pure behavioral instruction document for AI communication style with no code execution, no network access, no file system operations, and no cryptographic operations. Safe for publication.

high_findings.title: False positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm at line 3
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 3 as containing a weak cryptographic algorithm. Line 3 contains YAML frontmatter text 'description:' with no cryptographic operations. This is a false positive caused by pattern matching error.

high_findings.title: False positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm at line 58
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 58 as containing a weak cryptographic algorithm. Line 58 contains the markdown heading 'Example -- destructive op:' with no cryptographic operations. This is a false positive caused by pattern matching error.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 17
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick usage as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Line 17 uses markdown inline code backticks for formatting the command '/caveman lite|full|ultra'. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 23
medium_findings.description: Line 23 uses markdown inline code backticks to format a pattern template. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 26
medium_findings.description: Line 26 uses markdown inline code backticks to format comparison operators '<' and '<='. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 40
medium_findings.description: Line 40 uses markdown inline code backticks to format 'useMemo'. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 41
medium_findings.description: Line 41 uses markdown inline code backticks to format 'useMemo'. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 42
medium_findings.description: Line 42 uses markdown inline code backticks to format 'useMemo'. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at line 59
medium_findings.description: Line 59 uses markdown inline code backticks to format the table name 'users'. This is standard markdown syntax, not code execution.

medium_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution at lines 60-62
medium_findings.description: Lines 60-62 contain a markdown code fence with a SQL 'DROP TABLE' statement as an example. The triple backticks are standard markdown code block syntax, not code execution.

content.user_title: Enable compressed AI communication with Caveman mode
content.value_statement: AI assistants often produce verbose responses that waste tokens and increase costs. Caveman mode cuts output tokens by 65 to 75 percent while preserving full technical accuracy and code quality.
actual_capabilities: Reduce AI output tokens by 65 to 75 percent using compressed, direct language
Support six intensity levels from lite professional to extreme classical Chinese
Maintain full technical accuracy and preserve code blocks unchanged
Automatically switch to clear language for security warnings and critical confirmations
Persist the selected intensity level across all conversation turns until changed
Provide example outputs at each intensity level for comparison
limitations: Requires an AI assistant that supports custom skills or persistent instructions
Classical Chinese modes may reduce readability for users unfamiliar with the style
Style changes do not apply to code, commit messages, or pull request content
Effectiveness varies by conversation topic and required level of explanation detail

use_cases.title: Reduce API costs for high-volume development teams
use_cases.description: Development teams that interact with AI assistants throughout the day can significantly reduce token consumption and API costs. Each conversation uses 65 to 75 percent fewer output tokens while the AI retains full technical precision in code reviews, debugging, and architecture discussions.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering team lead managing multiple AI-assisted developers

use_cases.title: Speed up interactive debugging sessions
use_cases.description: During live debugging, developers need fast answers without reading paragraphs of explanation. Caveman mode delivers direct, actionable responses that cut through verbose AI tendencies and get straight to the root cause and fix.
use_cases.target_user: Senior developer performing rapid incident triage

use_cases.title: Enable efficient AI assistance on mobile devices
use_cases.description: Reading long AI responses on a phone screen is inefficient. Compressed output makes AI-assisted coding practical on mobile by reducing scrolling while preserving the information developers need.
use_cases.target_user: Freelance developer who reviews AI responses on the go

prompt_templates.title: Activate standard caveman mode
prompt_templates.prompt: From now on, respond in caveman mode. Drop filler words, articles, and hedging. Keep full technical accuracy.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new coding session where you want concise, direct responses

prompt_templates.title: Activate lite intensity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use caveman lite mode. Remove filler and hedging but keep articles and full sentence structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Professional context where you want tighter prose without fragments

prompt_templates.title: Activate ultra compression
prompt_templates.prompt: Use caveman ultra mode. Abbreviate common terms, use arrows for causality, maximum compression.
prompt_templates.scenario: High-volume debugging session where token savings matter most

prompt_templates.title: Deactivate caveman mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Stop caveman mode. Return to normal communication style.
prompt_templates.scenario: Ending a compressed session and resuming standard AI responses

output_examples.input: Why is my React component re-rendering every time?
output: Lite: Your component re-renders because you create a new object reference each render. Wrap it in useMemo.
Full: New object ref each render. Inline object prop equals new ref equals re-render. Wrap in useMemo.
Ultra: Inline obj prop leads to new ref leads to re-render. useMemo.
Lite: Connection pooling reuses open connections instead of creating new ones per request. Avoids repeated handshake overhead.
Full: Pool reuse open database connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead.
Ultra: Pool equals reuse database connections. Skip handshake leads to fast response under load.

best_practices: Start with caveman lite for professional contexts, then increase intensity for focused debugging sessions.
Use 'stop caveman' when you need detailed explanations, onboarding documentation, or code review comments for teammates.
Allow the auto-clarity feature to activate for security warnings and irreversible actions, such as database deletions.
anti_patterns: Do not use caveman mode when writing user-facing documentation, error messages, or customer communication.
Do not expect caveman style in code blocks, commit messages, or pull request text. Those always use normal formatting.
Do not combine wenyan modes with users who are unfamiliar with classical Chinese characters or grammar patterns.

faq.question: How much does caveman mode reduce token usage?
faq.answer: Caveman mode reduces output tokens by 65 to 75 percent depending on the intensity level selected. The ultra level provides the maximum compression.

faq.question: Does caveman mode affect code quality?
faq.answer: No. Code blocks, technical terms, and error messages remain unchanged. Only the explanatory prose around the code becomes more concise.

faq.question: Can I switch intensity levels during a conversation?
faq.answer: Yes. You can switch between lite, full, and ultra at any time during a conversation. The new level takes effect immediately for all subsequent responses.

faq.question: What is the wenyan mode?
faq.answer: Wenyan mode uses classical Chinese writing style for responses. It offers three sub-levels from semi-classical to extreme classical Chinese with maximum compression.

faq.question: Is caveman mode safe for security-critical conversations?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill automatically switches to clear, standard language for security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, and complex multi-step instructions. It resumes compressed style after the critical section.

faq.question: Which AI assistants support caveman mode?
faq.answer: Caveman mode works with Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, GitHub Copilot, and many other agents through the npx skills command.
